does: This paper introduces HAZDIAL, a framework that uses structured multi-agent dialogue to improve hazard identification in safety-critical systems. Rather than relying on single-turn LLM inference, the system engages multiple agents in iterative interactions—either adversarial debate or constructive discussion—to refine hazard identification outputs. The authors systematically evaluate these dialogue modalities against a curated golden dataset, measuring performance through standard classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) and novel dialogue-specific metrics. The core contribution demonstrates that multi-turn agentic interactions with self-correction and contextual refinement produce higher-quality hazard identification than monolithic single-pass baselines in domains like industrial process control and autonomous systems.
connects: This work builds on two converging lines of research. First, it extends recent efforts applying LLMs to safety-critical tasks and automated hazard analysis, addressing the brittleness of single-inference approaches. Second, it draws from the growing literature on multi-agent reasoning and dialogue systems for improving LLM outputs, following work on chain-of-thought reasoning, debate mechanisms for disagreement resolution, and interactive refinement in complex problem-solving. The paper specifically engages with dialogue-based approaches to AI reasoning developed in recent multi-agent frameworks, while applying these techniques to the specialized domain of operational safety where iterative deliberation mirrors how human safety engineers actually conduct hazard analysis through discussion and refinement.
enables: This framework opens several pathways for future work. It demonstrates that structured dialogue can enhance safety-critical NLP applications, potentially enabling broader adoption of LLM-assisted analysis in high-stakes domains by making systems more interpretable and trustworthy. The distinction between adversarial and constructive dialogue modalities suggests opportunities to optimize interaction patterns for different safety scenarios. The novel dialogue metrics introduced could become standard evaluation tools for assessing multi-agent reasoning quality beyond hazard identification. Future work might explore integrating HAZDIAL into real operational pipelines, investigating how dialogue-based approaches scale to complex industrial systems, and developing adaptive strategies that select dialogue modalities based on specific hazard types or domain characteristics.
